splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing of assumption selection directly controls the amount withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be selected before or by the statutory measurement date (end of prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability about potential liability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data even if it increases unpredictability for employers.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, extending the period during which violations can be found and revocation proceedings initiated.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to extend the term beyond what Congress prescribed.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits relitigating any previously presented claim — in addition to the separate requirements of § 2255(h). If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from raising any previously presented claim; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the § 2244(b)(1) bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, imposing a categorical bar in addition to § 2255(h) requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s gatekeeping requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting rules based on theories of competitive injury or vote dilution. The outcome determines whether election administration challenges can proceed before elections or must wait until after the fact, with significant implications for mail-in voting rules and ballot-counting deadlines nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates suffer a cognizable competitive injury from state election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes, giving them Article III standing to challenge such rules pre-election.

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not suffer a sufficiently concrete and particularized injury from general election rules to establish Article III standing for pre-election challenges.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one of the criteria is disqualifying. This split leads to dramatically disparate sentencing outcomes depending on jurisdiction, with defendants having identical criminal histories sentenced differently based on where they are prosecuted.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive (all three required)
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive (any one disqualifies)
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether lenity trumps deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails, while the Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity. The issue has taken on added significance after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor or Chevron deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference prevails over the rule of lenity, allowing authoritative interpretations of ambiguous guidelines to control even in the criminal context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses like conspiracy to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference and can expand the career-offender guideline beyond its text.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary adding conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' is a valid, authoritative interpretation under Stinson/Kisor, or impermissibly expands the guideline's text. This split affects the sentencing of numerous defendants whose prior convictions are for conspiracy or attempt rather than completed drug offenses.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses is a permissible interpretation of the ambiguous guideline text and is entitled to controlling weight under Stinson and Kisor.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so the commentary cannot add them; the note is not entitled to deference because it conflicts with the plain text.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them as one factor among others. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to allow this, but some circuits have held the amendment exceeds the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A), whether alone or combined with other factors.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as one factor among others in determining whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' encompasses state drug convictions involving substances not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether state drug offenses qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) when the state criminalizes substances not controlled under federal law. Some circuits read the guideline broadly to include any state drug offense, while others require the substance to be federally scheduled. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State offenses qualify regardless of federal schedules
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2(b) broadly includes offenses under state law without requiring the substance to also be federally controlled, as there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under federal law, applying a categorical approach that requires a match with federal schedules.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions regarding the terms (but not fact or duration) of imprisonment must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring civil rights claims.
splits.description: Circuits have been split for decades on whether challenges to conditions of confinement or BOP administrative decisions (such as denial of prerelease custody under the First Step Act) must be brought as habeas corpus petitions or may be brought as civil rights actions under Bivens or the APA. The Eighth Circuit has acknowledged this longstanding and unresolved circuit split.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting confinement conditions that implicate the fact or duration of imprisonment must be brought exclusively through habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241.

positions.label: Civil rights claims also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights or APA claims to challenge conditions of confinement or BOP administrative decisions that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of custody.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Carmona v. U.S. Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order resolving a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, filed in a criminal case, is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, but acknowledged a lopsided circuit split on the issue.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case, the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in property equity belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs after filing but before conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that appreciation inures to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor. This split affects debtors who experience changes in property values during their Chapter 13 cases before converting to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity are 'property of the estate' under § 348(f)(1)(A) because the broad scope of § 541(a) means post-petition appreciation inures to the estate.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) limits the converted estate to property as of the original petition date, so post-petition appreciation belongs to the debtor, consistent with the statute's structure and legislative history.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly impose sentences at supervised release revocation proceedings to punish defendants for their violations, or whether revocation sentences must serve only reintegrative and public-safety purposes.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether supervised release revocation sentences may be imposed for punitive purposes. The Sixth Circuit allows courts to expressly punish defendants for supervised release violations, placing it at one extreme, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences must focus on reentry and public safety rather than retribution. This affects sentencing outcomes for hundreds of individuals facing revocation proceedings each year.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose revocation sentences to punish defendants for violations of supervised release conditions, as the statute does not limit the purposes of revocation sentencing.

positions.label: Punishment is impermissible
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation is not designed to punish but to facilitate reentry and protect the public, and that imposing revocation sentences for retributive purposes is error.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records in litigation is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be irreparably lost if disclosure occurs before final judgment. This creates potential tension with other circuits' approaches to the collateral order doctrine's application to sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a high-order value that cannot be vindicated after disclosure.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are generally not immediately appealable because they do not meet the collateral order doctrine's stringent requirements, and mandamus or other remedies may be adequate.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to address the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in the hospitality industry, indicating an unsettled or split legal framework regarding the standards for establishing discrimination liability and available remedies when patrons allege unfair treatment by business operators.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied certain standards for evaluating discrimination claims against hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently important or unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different or conflicting standards for evaluating discrimination claims under § 1981 in the context of service and hospitality establishments.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption provision, which bars state laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts state-law negligence claims brought against freight brokers when the motor carrier they selected causes injury. The split has significant implications for the trucking and logistics industries.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because broker selection of carriers relates to the broker's 'services' within the meaning of the statute.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because such traditional tort claims do not relate to brokers' prices, routes, or services in the relevant sense.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus relief — such as alleged legal errors in the underlying conviction — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for a sentence reduction under the compassionate release statute. Some circuits permit consideration while others categorically bar it.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts have discretion to consider a broad range of factors, including potential legal errors in the conviction or sentence, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for challenging the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances, particularly marijuana, is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and the nation's historical tradition of firearms regulation.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming those who pose a danger to themselves or others, including habitual substance users, supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) even after Bruen.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government cannot demonstrate a sufficient historical analogue for disarming non-violent drug users.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
